title: Lemon meringue pie
subtitle: Blind-baked shortcrust, sharp lemon curd filling, and a soft Italian meringue topping
excerpt: A 23 cm lemon meringue pie: a crisp shortcrust case blind-baked until golden, filled with a sharp cornflour-thickened lemon curd, and topped with a soft, glossy Italian meringue torched to colour. Serves 6–8.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the lemon cheese tart chapter documents a blind-baked pastry case filled with a sharp lemon curd made from eggs, butter, sugar, and lemon juice, cooked to a set on the hob. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's lemon tart section documents the cornflour-thickened lemon filling that produces a sliceable curd. The meringue topping of whisked egg whites and sugar is documented in British baking literature from the Victorian period. The specific Italian meringue method (hot sugar syrup beaten into egg whites) appears in domestic recipe books from the Edwardian period onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The blind-baked case and lemon filling can be made and refrigerated a day ahead. Add the meringue and colour it on the day of serving — meringue weeps if left overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven for blind bake; meringue coloured with a kitchen torch, not in the oven

recipeTools.notes: For the Italian meringue — the syrup must reach 121°C exactly.
Or a hand mixer plus a heatproof bowl. You need both hands free to pour the hot syrup.
For colouring the meringue. An oven grill can be used but is less controllable.

glossaryTerms.definition: Meringue made by pouring a hot sugar syrup at 121°C (firm ball stage) in a thin stream into whipped egg whites while continuing to beat. The hot syrup partially cooks the egg whites as it combines, producing a stable, glossy, marshmallow-like meringue that holds its shape without weeping and does not need to be baked to be safe to eat. It is more involved than French meringue (egg whites and sugar beaten cold) but considerably more stable — it will not collapse or weep for several hours after being applied to the tart.

content.text: Lemon meringue pie is one of three components, each of which needs to be right: a shortcrust case that stays crisp and doesn't shrink; a lemon filling that is properly sharp and slices cleanly; and a meringue that stays glossy and doesn't weep.
solves the weeping problem definitively. It requires a sugar thermometer and a steady pour, but it is stable for hours where French meringue softens and separates within 30 minutes.

items.prepNote: juice and zest — about 150 ml juice

content.text: Make and blind bake the case

Rub together {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} until it resembles fine crumbs. Mix {{egg-yolks}} with {{water}} cold water and add just enough to bring the dough together. Chill for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Roll to 3 mm, line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin, prick the base, line with baking paper and beans, and blind bake for 15 minutes. Remove beans and paper and bake for a further 10 minutes until the base is deep golden and completely crisp. Cool in the tin.

Whisk together {{caster-sugar}}, {{cornflour}}, and {{water}} in a saucepan until smooth. Add the {{lemon}} lemon juice and zest. Heat over medium heat, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens and comes to a boil — it will turn from cloudy to thick and translucent. Remove from the heat. Beat the {{egg-yolks}} briefly and stir into the hot lemon mixture off the heat. Return to low heat and stir for 1 minute more. Remove from heat and beat in the {{unsalted-butter}} butter cubes until melted and smooth. Pour into the cooled tart case. Press cling film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold and set, at least 1 hour.

Dissolve {{caster-sugar}} in {{water}} in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil without stirring and cook until the syrup reaches 121°C on a sugar thermometer. While the syrup is climbing in temperature, begin whisking the {{egg-whites}} egg whites to soft peaks in a stand mixer on high speed. When the syrup hits 121°C, remove from the heat and pour it in a thin, steady stream down the side of the bowl into the whisking egg whites — avoid the beaters. Continue whisking until the bowl is cool to the touch and the meringue is stiff, glossy, and holds peaks, about 5 minutes.

Pile the meringue onto the set lemon filling, spreading it right to the pastry edge to seal (this prevents the meringue sliding). Swirl the surface into peaks. Use a kitchen torch to colour the meringue to a golden brown. Serve within 2 hours.
